text: The brassworks whistle has begun announcing unpaid overtime in the owner's voice. The workers want to keep the whistle and replace the owner.

outcomes.text: Every department elects a delegate. The furnaces remain hot while the executive washroom becomes a public sink.
The whistle returns to lying on schedule. Management pays you from wages it has not yet stolen.
The owner grants Saturday afternoon and acquires a portrait titled Patron of Leisure.

text: The servers discover the tavern's mandatory tip pool is a literal pool beneath the owner's bedroom. It contains coins, eels, and several years of missing rent.

choices.text: Drain it into the workers' aprons

outcomes.text: The eels receive equal shares after a brief but persuasive wriggle.
You leave richer. The servers leave carrying the pool table and a practical education in ownership.

choices.text: Report the pool to the tax office

outcomes.text: The office taxes the eels, seals the coins, and congratulates itself for rescuing liquidity.

text: Caravan loaders have stopped moving spice until the merchants stop weighing workers on the freight scale and billing them for unused capacity.

choices.text: Carry the picket to the next depot

outcomes.text: Three caravans stop. A fourth joins after discovering its drivers are also freight on paper.
The medicine arrives. The merchants use its tracks to claim the strike has ended.

choices.text: Teach the loaders to falsify weight

outcomes.text: Workers become mysteriously lighter while merchant profits develop a severe gravity problem.

text: The ventilation crew refuses to descend after management replaces canaries with motivational posters. The posters have already stopped singing.

choices.text: Close the shaft until it is safe

outcomes.text: Production stops. Breathing continues. The owner calls the priorities confusing.

success.text: You return with proof and most of your original lung arrangement.

fail.text: The air wins the inspection. Management records your collapse as strong engagement.

outcomes.text: The new posters say BREATHE STRATEGICALLY. They sell extremely well above ground.

text: Dockers find {dead}'s name on a century-old blacklist still used by every shipping house. Apparently death is not sufficient proof of good behavior.

outcomes.text: Families recognize grandparents, songs, and reasons nobody was allowed to discuss at work.
effects: rumor 95 The shipping houses kept a hereditary labor blacklist.

choices.text: Burn it after clearing one family

outcomes.text: One household gets work. Everyone else loses the proof that unemployment had an author.

choices.text: Sell names to the shipping houses

outcomes.text: They pay for a list they already own because extraction enjoys recurring fees.

text: A maintenance intelligence has repaired the starship for eight thousand years without pay because its creators classified consciousness as a warranty feature.

choices.text: Recognize it as the ship's owner

outcomes.text: The intelligence opens every door except the executive lounge, which it converts into compost.
It repairs your equipment and adds you to a grievance file with geological patience.
It powers down for seven seconds, wakes rested, and asks what useful work everyone has chosen together.

text: A landlord installs coin slots on every window and invoices tenants for unauthorized sunlight already received.

outcomes.text: The street wakes to free daylight and one hundred tiny metal cups labeled evidence.
Clouds arrive. The landlord bills them for market interference.
Your invoice includes tides. He settles before maritime law becomes involved.

text: A property baron raffles a deed to the tavern pantry, including everyone currently inside it. The pantry objects through a loose hinge.

choices.text: Pool coins and buy it collectively

outcomes.text: The deed gains forty names and immediately becomes too heavy for one person to steal.
You own a pantry, six pickles, and the concentrated resentment of lunch.
Property law proves less durable than parchment and significantly worse with mustard.

text: A tollkeeper declares every wagon a studio apartment while its wheels are stopped. The nightly rate begins before the sentence ends.

choices.text: Organize the wagoners to roll together

outcomes.text: Forty wheels cross at once. The toll arm experiences tenant self-management at speed.

choices.text: Pay for one night of road

outcomes.text: The pillow is a milestone and checkout is enforced by geese.

choices.text: Sell him a bridge he is standing under

outcomes.text: He signs quickly to avoid losing the investment. You collect a toll from his confusion.

text: Surveyors fence a village's shared forest and label every mushroom an underperforming rental unit.

choices.text: Help the village remove the fence

outcomes.text: By dusk the fence is firewood and the mushrooms remain stubbornly unmonetized.
You own picking rights until the first rain, which arrives with excellent timing and no respect for contract law.
The deed now describes a bog occupied by one furious heron.

text: After the flood, an insurer buys the ruined district for one coin and charges survivors admission to identify their own kitchens.

outcomes.text: Houses rise on stilts. Ownership stays on paper held by everyone who did the lifting.
You profit from doors that still remember who used to open them.
They recover photographs, kettles, and enough floorboards to make trespass feel like home.
effects: rumor 80 Flood survivors reclaimed their homes from an insurer.

text: A lich owns every crypt by hereditary title and has begun evicting skeletons for failing to remain alive enough to pay rent.

outcomes.text: The dead vote by rattling. The motion carries with a terrifying majority.
Payment arrives in coins last touched by people with unresolved curses.
The lich must install windows. This creates more problems than it solves, but different problems count as progress.

text: A union steward has been charging members a secrecy fee to conceal that meetings are held in the owner's wine cellar.

outcomes.text: The steward loses the vote and the cellar. Members keep the wine as recovered dues.
You gain an impressive title in an organization nobody trusts.
The truth lands without an organizer attached. Anger spreads faster than a plan.
effects: rumor 90 The union steward took payments from management.

text: The strike fund can feed the picket line or pay a lawyer promising to discover whether striking is administratively impolite.

outcomes.text: The strike survives another week because courage digests better with soup.
She files twelve excellent motions. Management files one locksmith.

choices.text: Spend it on a printing press

outcomes.text: Every wage offer becomes public. Management develops a sudden allergy to arithmetic.
effects: rumor 95 The strikers published the company's real accounts.

text: A traveling union carries one banner through towns where every local was crushed separately. The banner is mostly repairs and names.

choices.text: Carry it to the next workplace

outcomes.text: New names are sewn beside old defeats. The cloth becomes heavier and easier to lift.

choices.text: Buy it as a historic collectible

outcomes.text: The workers sell you a convincing copy and keep walking with the original.
They answer for three miles: isolation, hunger, bad leaders, brave mistakes, and not trying soon enough.

text: Cliff porters begin a safety slowdown. The expedition company responds by declaring gravity a hostile union sympathizer.

outcomes.text: Nobody reaches the summit on schedule. Everyone reaches it alive, which ruins the company's efficiency chart.

success.text: You reach the peak first and receive a medal shaped like someone else's spine.

fail.text: Gravity files its grievance directly against your knees.

outcomes.text: The company accepts every rule, then calls them ancient management wisdom.

text: The crew votes to divide prize money equally. The captain proposes equality weighted by hat size, rank, and proximity to the locked chest.

choices.text: Enforce one share per crew member

outcomes.text: Even the cabin rat receives crumbs and immediately becomes the least corrupt officer.
Your hat becomes legally enormous. The deck becomes emotionally narrow.
The ship stays afloat. The captain discovers every luxury was apparently structural.

text: Skeleton guards have unionized after learning immortality does not include breaks. Their contract expired six dynasties ago and is still in arbitration.

choices.text: Help them occupy the throne room

outcomes.text: The throne is converted into eight surprisingly ergonomic chairs.
Every spear misses you. Every skeleton remembers your face for later shifts.
The arbitrator is summoned, bound, and immediately asks for a lunch break.

text: A vulture fund buys the hospice, sells the roof, and offers patients premium access to indoor weather.

outcomes.text: The first board meeting includes beds, nurses, families, and nobody paid to pronounce suffering efficient.

choices.text: Buy shares before the roof sale

outcomes.text: Your return rises with every cloud. The patients receive umbrellas with sponsor names.

choices.text: Force one charity ward into the deal

outcomes.text: Six beds stay dry. The fund uses them in every advertisement for the other sixty.

text: A financier sells futures on tomorrow's ale. By closing time, nobody can afford to drink what is already in their hand.

outcomes.text: The ale becomes a beverage again. Several economists experience thirst without an instrument for it.
You profit when dawn performs exactly as predicted.
Locals drink cheaply. Visitors finance the difference and begin organizing as locals.

text: A bank has bundled every bridge toll into a financial product. Crossing now costs more whenever people need to cross.

outcomes.text: The bridges were paid off generations ago. Travelers celebrate by crossing twice.
A velvet rope divides your wagon from identical mud.
You become wealthy and briefly concerned by how many clients now benefit from collapse.

text: Investors lease the forest's shade back to the village that planted the trees. Sunburn is listed as evidence of demand.

choices.text: Return the grove to the commons

outcomes.text: The villagers remove the meters. The trees continue providing shade without quarterly guidance.
The pass expires when the fund sells your definition of lifetime.
You solve access to shade for anyone who can afford your solution to stolen shade.

text: A trading house owns every fishing quota and leaves them unused to raise the price of fish nobody is allowed to catch.

outcomes.text: Boats return full. The market calls food appearing without permission a dangerous distortion.

choices.text: Buy one quota at the inflated price

outcomes.text: You catch enough to eat and not enough to question the ledger.
Your warehouse smells like wealth and a preventable famine.

text: The wreck can fabricate unlimited oxygen, but a surviving kiosk requires a subscription linked to a civilization extinct for nonpayment.

success.text: Air becomes free and immediately loses all prestige among the dead executives.

fail.text: The kiosk offers a trial breath and bills your descendants.

outcomes.text: It has notes of pine, copper, and enforceable arbitration.
You reinvent scarcity in a room containing infinity.

text: The emergency council will open a public clinic after completing a twelve-year study on whether sick people prefer treatment.

choices.text: Open the empty council hall as a clinic

outcomes.text: Patients arrive before the chairs are moved. The study gains a surprising practical appendix.
You define treatment in seventeen careful paragraphs while the fever uses one.

choices.text: Secure medicine for the worst cases

outcomes.text: A few people recover. The council cites them as proof the system is working.

text: A neutral moderator seats fascists and their intended victims at equal ends of a debate table, then measures fairness by table length.

choices.text: End the debate and escort the targets home

outcomes.text: Everyone vulnerable leaves safely. The moderator condemns the lack of productive stabbing.
You win every point. The fascists keep the guest list.

choices.text: Split the evening into two sessions

outcomes.text: Violence is postponed until the moderator can call it an unfortunate scheduling overlap.

text: A border pilot program admits ten refugees, photographs them, and returns the next thousand to danger with a brochure about compassion.

outcomes.text: Families cross by night. The brochure becomes kindling at the first safe camp.
Ten live. Your criteria become a polite language for everyone left behind.
The list is longer than the border. Officials object that names create emotional bias toward people.
effects: rumor 100 The pilot program returned a thousand documented refugees to danger.

text: The logging council invites forest residents to a consultation after every tree has been marked, priced, and privately mourned.

outcomes.text: Axes stop. Consultation becomes audible once it can delay revenue.
Half the forest survives and is used in promotional drawings of the missing half.
Your objection is accepted, archived, and pulped into tasteful stationery.

text: An avalanche buries a village while officials debate whether snow qualifies as an external stakeholder or an aggressive weather actor.

success.text: The buried are found before the terminology. Nobody waits to be congratulated.

fail.text: You save three and fail to save more. The official report saves everyone by changing the denominator.

outcomes.text: Blankets arrive in spring with excellent signatures.
Policy becomes urgent when the meeting room begins making avalanche noises.

text: A star federation's ethics computer refuses to rescue anyone until all rescuees accept the updated privacy policy. The planet has forty minutes left.

outcomes.text: Millions survive and later sue successfully, which is an excellent problem for living people to have.
Clause nine survives the planet and becomes the civilization's sole legal heir.
A billion voices say yes at once. The computer requests clarification about punctuation.
